---
title: "Chapter title"
description: "One sentence stating the chapter's engineering purpose."
chapter-id: Z00
status: outline
---

<!--
Keep the opening `Central question` and `Learning outcomes` headings and the
closing `Exercises`, `Connections`, and `References` headings. The other headings
below name editorial FUNCTIONS. Retitle them for this chapter. Each heading
should name a specific concept, mechanism, operating region, or decision. See
authoring/README.md and authoring/technical-style.md for voice, pacing, diagrams,
code listings, and citations.
-->

## Central question

Open with an observable behavior, discrepancy, failure, or real design need.
Collect a prediction before introducing the governing idea.

Define the central technical term in **bold** and plain language when it first
appears. Use direct, active sentences. If the chapter moves from a component to
a circuit or system, write the bridge explicitly.

## Learning outcomes

Write three to five observable outcomes. Name the prerequisite idea that each
outcome builds on. Avoid generic outcomes that could be pasted into every
chapter.

## Develop the central idea

Define quantities, reference directions, units, assumptions, and the domain of
validity. Connect the physical view, circuit or architecture, words, and
equations. Place a circuit diagram beside topology, a waveform beside time
behavior, and a graph or table beside an operating trend. Tell the reader what
to inspect before each visual and interpret it afterward.

## Work an example

Estimate, calculate, check units and limiting cases, then interpret the result.

## Reconcile with evidence

Compare prediction, simulation where useful, and measured or authentic data.
Record configuration, raw evidence, and uncertainty. Keep code listings short so
each line wraps within the page.

## Where it breaks down

Develop non-ideal effects, common misconceptions, and one plausible fault through
discriminating evidence.

## Make a design decision

Give measurable requirements, alternatives, trade-offs, an acceptance test, and
the limit of the resulting claim.

## Summary

Connect the chapter's central idea to its downstream chapters.

## Exercises

Open with a short multiple-choice quick check — one best answer each, followed by
an answer key — aimed at the chapter's common misconceptions. Then include
retrieval, estimation, derivation, data interpretation, debugging, and open
design. Keep live-assessment variants and full solutions outside the public book.

## Connections

Link prerequisites, downstream chapters, aligned labs and projects, and
just-in-time appendices by ID. State what each connection contributes.

## References

Cite claims at the point of use and prefer primary technical sources for
version-bound or consequential facts.
